name: Three Villages Trail
name_original: Sendero de los Tres Pueblos (PR-A 360) — Las Canales de Padules por Beires y Almócita
description: Hike the moderate 6.4-mile Sendero de los Tres Pueblos (PR-A 360) through Alpujarran villages and the stunning Las Canales de Padules gorge.
region_intro: Hiking around Padules offers access to a diverse landscape shaped by the Andarax River, nestled between the Sierra de Gádor and Sierra Nevada mountain ranges. The region is characterized by spectacular gorges like Las Canales de Padules, featuring waterfalls, natural pools, and unique rock formations. Lush riparian vegetation contrasts with surrounding crags, providing a rich environment for outdoor exploration. This area provides varied terrain for hikers of all skill levels. Best hiking trails around Padules The most popular hiking route is Three Villages Trail, a 6.4 miles (10.3 km) trail that takes 3 hours 7 minutes to complete. This moderate route meanders through traditional Alpujarran villages and the Las Canales de Padules gorge. Another top favourite among local hikers is Danger of Falling Canyon – The Canals of Padules loop from Padules, a moderate 5.4 miles (8.7 km) path. This trail offers an immersive experience of the gorge, with its narrow passages and natural pools. Local hikers also love the Grandma's Little Mill loop from Padules, a 1.5 miles (2.4 km) trail leading through river valleys and natural pools, often completed in about 45 minutes. Hiking around Padules is defined by deep gorges, natural pools, and river valleys. The network offers options for easy, moderate, and more challenging routes, catering to various ability levels. The routes in Padules are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.4 stars from over 60 reviews. More than 300 hikers have used komoot to explore Padules's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many hiking trails are available around Padules?
A: There are nearly 30 hiking trails around Padules, offering a variety of experiences for different skill levels. You'll find options ranging from easy strolls to more challenging routes through the region's unique landscapes.

Q: Are there easy hiking options suitable for beginners or families in Padules?
A: Yes, Padules offers several easy hiking options perfect for beginners or families. For instance, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/10304183" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Grandma's Little Mill loop from Padules&lt;/a&gt; is a shorter, easy trail leading through river valleys and natural pools, typically completed in under an hour. There are 18 easy routes in total.

Q: What natural features can I expect to see on hikes around Padules?
A: Hikes around Padules are renowned for their spectacular natural features, primarily centered around &lt;strong&gt;Las Canales de Padules&lt;/strong&gt;. This gorge, carved by the Andarax River, boasts waterfalls, natural pools perfect for refreshing dips, and unique rock formations. You can also explore the narrow &lt;strong&gt;Los Canjorros canyon&lt;/strong&gt;, where the rock walls are incredibly close. Lush riparian vegetation contrasts beautifully with the surrounding crags, offering a vibrant landscape. Don't miss highlights like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2967761" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;The Canals of Padules&lt;/a&gt; itself.

Q: Are there any circular hiking routes in the Padules area?
A: Yes, many of the trails around Padules are circular, allowing you to start and end in the same location. A popular example is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/21832293" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Danger of Falling Canyon – The Canals of Padules loop from Padules&lt;/a&gt;, which offers an immersive experience of the gorge. Another option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/39837799" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Danger of Falling Canyon – The Canals of Padules loop from Almócita&lt;/a&gt;, providing a different starting point for exploring the same stunning area.

Q: Can I hike with my dog on the trails near Padules?
A: While specific regulations for dogs on every trail can vary, the natural environment around Padules, with its open spaces and river access, is generally welcoming for dogs. It's always recommended to keep your dog on a leash, especially in protected natural areas like Las Canales de Padules, and to ensure you clean up after them. Always check local signage for any specific restrictions.

Q: What is the best time of year to go hiking in Padules?
A: The best time to hike in Padules is generally during the spring (March to May) and autumn (September to November) months. During these periods, the weather is milder and more pleasant for outdoor activities. Summer can be very hot, making early morning or late afternoon hikes preferable, especially if you plan to enjoy the natural pools. Winter offers a different, often quieter, experience, though some paths might be cooler or wetter.

Q: What do other hikers enjoy most about the trails in Padules?
A: The komoot community highly rates the trails in Padules, with an average score of 4.4 stars from over 60 reviews. Hikers frequently praise the stunning natural beauty of Las Canales de Padules, the refreshing natural pools, and the unique experience of river hiking. The diverse terrain and the contrast between lush riverbeds and rocky crags are also often highlighted as major draws.

Q: Are there any historical or cultural attractions to visit in Padules village?
A: Beyond the natural landscapes, the village of Padules itself offers cultural insights. You can explore traditional Alpujarran architecture with its whitewashed houses and narrow streets. Notable historical sites include the 16th-century &lt;strong&gt;Church of Santa María la Mayor&lt;/strong&gt; and the &lt;strong&gt;Cross of Peace&lt;/strong&gt;, which commemorates the end of the Alpujarra War. The village's rich history, including its Hispano-Roman and Arab origins, adds another layer of interest to your visit.

Q: Are there opportunities for activities other than traditional hiking in Padules?
A: Yes, the unique environment of Las Canales de Padules supports several other outdoor activities. &lt;strong&gt;River hiking&lt;/strong&gt; is a popular option, allowing adventurers to wade through shallow waters and explore the gorge from within. The impressive rock facades also make the area popular for &lt;strong&gt;rock climbing&lt;/strong&gt; and &lt;strong&gt;abseiling&lt;/strong&gt;, with guided tours often available for those seeking more adventurous pursuits.

Q: Is there parking available for hikers in Padules?
A: Yes, parking is generally available in and around the village of Padules, especially near popular trailheads. As it's a small village, designated parking areas might be limited, but you can usually find spots within walking distance of the main routes. It's always a good idea to arrive early during peak seasons to secure a convenient parking space.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on the hiking trails around Padules?
A: The hiking trails around Padules feature diverse terrain. You'll encounter paths winding through deep gorges, alongside the Andarax River, and through lush river valleys. Some sections involve rocky paths, especially within the canyons, while others might be softer earth. The elevation changes are moderate on many routes, offering a good workout without being overly strenuous, with the landscape transitioning between the Sierra de Gádor and Sierra Nevada mountain ranges.
tips: Almócita, a town with Arab roots founded by the descendants of Tubal, is known for the parish church of Our Lady of Mercy. The urban structure preserves traces of the Arab era, with streets that betray its origins. The Lower Quarter houses the old Jewish Quarter and the Moorish architecture in the city center is the best preserved in the province.

From this point you can see the charming town of Almócita. The town captivates with its picturesque alleys and colorful flower pots. Part of the Three Towns Path, this Almeria jewel, with Roman roots, joins the declaration of Las Canales de Padules as a Natural Monument. History, beauty and authenticity are intertwined in this corner of the Alpujarras.

The Andarax river is born in a town very close to Padules, this town is Laujar de Andarax. This well-known river in our province passes through Padules and its section is a route full of nooks, falls, pools and canyons. All this route receives the name of Los Canales de Padules.
Recreational area with picnic tables and good shade. A perfect place to make a stop during the Tres Pueblos Route (PR-A 36).

faqs.question: Where can I park to start the Sendero de los Tres Pueblos (PR-A 360)?
faqs.answer: The Sendero de los Tres Pueblos is a circular route, so you can typically start and end in the same location. Common starting points with parking options are the villages of Almócita or Padules. It's advisable to check local signage upon arrival for designated parking areas within these villages.

faqs.question: What is the terrain like on this hiking route?
faqs.answer: The route features varied terrain, including natural footpaths, forest tracks, and some paved sections. A significant part, especially within Las Canales de Padules, involves traversing a riverbed with gravel and pebbles. Be prepared for uneven ground and potentially wading through shallow water in certain gorge sections.

faqs.question: How difficult is the Sendero de los Tres Pueblos (PR-A 360)?
faqs.answer: This trail is generally considered to be of moderate difficulty. While not overly challenging for experienced hikers, some sections might be demanding for those with less experience due to uneven terrain and occasional less-than-optimal signage. It's not recommended for young children under six or individuals with reduced mobility.

faqs.question: What are the main points of interest along the trail?
faqs.answer: The main highlight is undoubtedly Las Canales de Padules, a stunning gorge with crystal-clear pools. You'll also experience the charm of the three villages: Almócita, Padules, and Beires, with their traditional architecture and cultural insights. Don't miss the Picnic Area on the Ruta de Tres Pueblos for a break.

faqs.question: Is the Sendero de los Tres Pueblos (PR-A 360) dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: Yes, the trail is generally dog-friendly. However, given that parts of the route pass through villages and potentially near livestock, it's always best to keep your dog on a leash. Be mindful of other hikers and the natural environment.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to hike this trail?
faqs.answer: The best time to hike this route is typically during the spring (April-May) and autumn (September-October) when temperatures are milder. Summer can be very hot, though the riverbed sections in Las Canales de Padules offer refreshing dips. Winter can be pleasant, but check weather conditions for potential rain or colder temperatures.

faqs.question: Do I need a permit or pay an entrance fee for this trail?
faqs.answer: No, you do not need a specific permit or to pay an entrance fee to hike the Sendero de los Tres Pueblos (PR-A 360). However, as the trail is located within the Sierra Nevada Natural Park, it's important to respect park regulations regarding waste, noise, and protecting flora and fauna.

faqs.question: Are there any specific regulations for hiking in the Sierra Nevada Natural Park?
faqs.answer: Yes, as part of the Sierra Nevada Natural Park, hikers should adhere to general park regulations. This includes staying on marked paths, not disturbing wildlife, carrying out all trash, and refraining from making fires. Always respect private property when passing through cultivated areas.

faqs.question: Does the trail intersect other well-known paths?
faqs.answer: Yes, the route intersects with several other local trails. You will find sections that overlap with the Sendero de Almócita, Bogaraya - Cacín - Minas de la Solana, and Molino del Río y Fuente de los Naranjos, among others.

faqs.question: Are there amenities like food and water available along the route?
faqs.answer: The trail connects the villages of Almócita, Padules, and Beires, where you can find amenities such as small shops, cafes, and restaurants. It's always a good idea to carry sufficient water, especially during warmer months, as services might be limited between villages.

faqs.question: What should I wear and bring for this hike?
faqs.answer: Given the varied terrain, including riverbed sections, wear sturdy hiking shoes or waterproof boots. Bring plenty of water, snacks, sun protection (hat, sunscreen), and layers of clothing as temperatures can change. A swimsuit might be nice for a dip in Las Canales de Padules during warmer weather.

waypoints.name: Picnic Area on the Ruta de Tres Pueblos
